M - Ladder deck bridge-CSi Bridge
content:
_Captured Saturday, 28 August 2021 8:57 AM · Computational › CSi Bridge_

**Diagrams**
![M - Ladder deck bridge](https://sattarfd.github.io/image-host/sof_008_m-ladder-deck-bridge-c_1.gif)
![M - Ladder deck bridge](https://sattarfd.github.io/image-host/sof_008_m-ladder-deck-bridge-c_2.gif)

- Sample model;
- SH43 Fixed_CEL
- Define user discretization points (in the bridge object) to force splitting of the deck shell to line up with the cross beam locations. This is not necessarily critical in this instance, but it does make things a bit tidier. However, because the software also splits the deck shell in line with supports, orthogonal user discretization points around the support become messy as they clash with the line of the pier.
- Manually define the cross beams which aren't generated by the bridge object (the orthogonal cross beams at the abutments - this is easiest done using the replicate function on the closest parametrically-defined cross beams. Then trim the new cross beams as required so they don't extend beyond abutment lines.
- Draw vertical rigid links between the cross beams and deck shell - in this instance we've created 3 links per cross beam at 1/4 points along the cross beam which is a bit coarse but refining that is just a matter of replicating more links once the first is drawn. Create a group for the top node of the rigid links ('GROUP1').
- Select the deck shell elements and change the auto mesh type as below, with the previously created group (for the top nodes of the rigid links) selected.
- We've done something similar to the above to capture where the orthogonal cross beams hit the girder webs near supports.
- The resulting mesh ends up looking as below.
- I've also updated the section cut definition to line up with one of the cross beams (noting that the quadrilateral cutting plane needs to be a vertical plane in this case). Another option for getting actions for composite members like this one would be to look at results for the cross beam member, multiply the axial load x eccentricity to the deck shell and sum with the moment from the results. This approach takes the guesswork out of exactly how much of the deck slab is effective.

- In order to assign a load to selected objects, a thermal load or imposed displacement point load for example, you first need to have a load pattern defined for it.
- Load pattern
- A
- load pattern
- is the spatial distribution of a specific set of forces, displacements, temperatures, and other effects which act on a structure. Any combination of
- joints
- and elements may be subjected to loading and kinematic conditions. Each load pattern is assigned a design type (DEAD, WIND, QUAKE, etc.) which classifies the load and initiates the associated computational process.
- Load case
- A
- load case
- defines how
- load patterns
- are applied (statically or dynamically), how the structure responds (linearly or nonlinearly), and how analysis is performed (through
- modal analysis
- ,
- direct integration
- , etc.).
- After a load pattern is defined, the Define>Load cases dialogue is where users specify whether the load pattern is analysed as linear or nonlinear, statically or dynamically, with or without P-delta, and where users define load sequencing and the combining of loads. This enables the same load pattern to be analysed in dif
- ferent ways.

title: 4b- Creating Surface - From Contour
content:
_Captured Thursday, 18 January 2024 11:56 PM · Computational › Civil 3D_

**Diagrams**
![4b- Creating Surface -](https://sattarfd.github.io/image-host/sof_063_4b-creating-surface-fr_1.png)

- Open your Civil 3D Project: Start Civil 3D and open the DWG file containing the contour lines.
- Open the ToolSpace: On the left side, you should see the ToolSpace. If it's not there, you can open it by clicking on the "Home" tab > "Palettes" panel > "ToolSpace".

**Create a New Surface**
- In the ToolSpace, go to the "Prospector" tab.
- Right-click on "Surfaces" > "Create Surface".
- Name the Surface: In the "Create Surface" dialog box, give your surface a name and description. Choose the appropriate style (e.g., "Contours 1' and 5'") and render material if needed. Click "OK".

**Add Contour Data to the Surface**
- In the ToolSpace, under "Prospector" > "Surfaces", you should now see your new surface listed. Expand it.
- Right-click on "Contours" > "Add". This will allow you to add the contour data from your DWG to the surface.
- Select the contour lines from your DWG. Once selected, press "Enter".
- Rebuild the Surface: After adding the contour data, the surface will automatically rebuild. You can see the surface in the Object Viewer or by using various surface styles.
- Review and Edit if Necessary: Once the surface is generated, you might need to make edits or adjustments. You can do this using the "Edit Surface" tools in the "Modify" tab.

title: Getting line coordination from Civil 3D
content:
_Captured Monday, 9 December 2024 4:36 pm · Computational › Dynamo_

- To define timber pole set-out locations relative to a feature line in Civil 3D using Dynamo, follow these steps:
- Step 1: Prepare the Feature Line in Civil 3D
- Ensure the feature line is properly defined and has the correct elevations in Civil 3D.
- Name the feature line for easier identification in Dynamo.
- Step 2: Set Up Dynamo for Civil 3D
- Open Dynamo for Civil 3D.
- Load the
- Civil 3D Toolkit
- package (if not already installed) from the Dynamo Package Manager. This provides nodes for interacting with Civil 3D elements.
- Step 3: Import the Feature Line
- Use the Select Civil 3D Feature Line node to select the feature line from Civil 3D.
- Extract the geometry of the feature line using Feature Line.GetGeometry.
- Retrieve the points along the feature line using Feature Line.Points.
- Step 4: Define Pile Spacing
- Decide on the spacing for the timber poles (e.g., every 2 meters along the feature line).
- Use a Range node to generate intervals along the length of the feature line.
- Step 5: Locate Set-Out Points
- Use the Curve.PointAtParameter node to calculate points along the feature line at specific intervals. The parameter input is based on a normalized value (0 to 1), so adjust your spacing accordingly.
- Convert real-world distances to normalized values using Curve.Length and appropriate calculations.
- Adjust the points laterally to define the pile locations:
- Use Point.Add or Point.Translate to shift the points perpendicular to the feature line (e.g., offset for wall alignment).
- Step 6: Generate Pile Locations
- Convert the points into coordinates that Civil 3D can use for timber pole placement.
- Use the Civil 3D Point.Create node to add the points into the Civil 3D drawing as markers for pile locations.
- Step 7: Verify and Adjust
- Return to Civil 3D to verify the pile locations.
- Adjust parameters in Dynamo as needed (e.g., spacing, offsets, alignment).
- Example Dynamo Workflow
- Input
- : Feature line from Civil 3D.
- Process

****
- Extract geometry.
- Define spacing and calculate intervals.
- Offset points laterally for the pile set-out.
- Output
- : Set-out points as Civil 3D points or AutoCAD entities.
- If you need a detailed step-by-step script or specific adjustments for your project, let me know!
- Install toolkit: Toolkit

**Import feature line**

title: Rigid link for vertical offset
content:
_Captured Wednesday, 16 June 2021 12:44 PM · Computational › LUSAS_

- Rigid link for vertical offset;
- To model the required vertical offset
- ,
- I would generally recommend the use of a rigid, mass-less thick beam (with say EI x 1000 compared to the connected structural elements and some nominal sectional properties) instead of using rigid link constraints.
- To start with you could just create a new immaterial with E x 1000 compared to concrete/steel and use one of the actual geometric line attributes for sectional properties.
- Constraint equations add complexity which can be an issue in larger models, so using the simplest approach possible is preferred.
- As per point 2) I would recommend thick beams are used at all connection points but please check appropriate mesh, geometric and material attributes have been assigned

- can be used to limit the area over which the presecribed load is applied so that the effect of the load on other features in the model may prevented. If a search area is not specified the default loading area is
- Whole model
- .
- Nodes in search area
- - restricts the application of the applied load to just those mesh nodes within the search area, or to the whole model, as appropriate.
- For shell/plate/grillage models
- , it is recommended that the DMI analysis should be defined with the
- ‘Nodes in the search area’
- selected. For line beam models
- where the geometric beam section represents a loadable top slab
- , it is recommended that the
- Grid option be used
- instead, specifying Grid settings to automatically create grid points to match the longitudinal positions of the nodes in the search area.
- Grid
- - predominantly used with line beam models where the geometric beam section represents a loadable top slab. For this, the grid of points is used to represent the loadable region of the slab. The Grid method also provides the means to define a grid of points that can be used as an alternative to the nodes or points present in a shell or plate model that are used in an influence analysis.
- For a model with a very fine mesh
- ,
- loading a grid having fewer points will reduce the solution time
- . For line beam models the use of a
- reference path
- using lines representing the centreline of a loaded region of a structure is recommended if the grid loading method is used. For plate and shell models a reference path may represent the centreline of a carriageway on which a loading grid would be positioned.
- Centreline
- - the centreline of the grid can be defined with reference to a previously defined
- Path
- or the
- X
- ,
- Y
- or
- Z
- global axes. For most types of modelling the use of a
- reference path
- is recommended. Specifying X, Y or Z causes the grid to be created along each of those axes at X=0, Y=0 or Z=0.
- Transverse width
- - the width of the loadable grid to be generated (must be greater than 0.1). Grid settings control the number of load locations within this defined width.
- Grid settings
- - These control the density of the grid of points that will be loaded. Longitudinal and transverse values can be specified. If one of the global axes has been selected as the centreline of the grid, then the overall extent of the model will be calculated and a suitable grid generated to encompass the whole model.

title: Negative eigenvalue
content:
_Captured Thursday, 23 April 2020 9:02 PM · Computational › LUSAS_

- Reference Documents;
- 1030_Negative_Eigenvalues
- How do I eliminate negative eigenvalues from an eigenvalue buckling analysis
- An eigenvalue buckling analysis calculates the linear buckling load factors.
- This means that if the applied loads are amplified by the given load factor then the structure buckles with a specific deformed shape (mode shape).
- It is important
- load factors are close to unity
- Generally when negative eigenvalues are calculated and the points mentioned in paragraph 2.3 (and the web page) have been investigated, the negative eigenvalues can be ignored and the first positive eigenvalue could be considered as correct, provided that the error norm is small.
- How do I eliminate negative eigenvalues from an eigenvalue buckling analysis?
- It is essential to perform a linear static analysis prior to the eigenvalue analysis. This eliminates the added complexities of the eigen-analysis and enables a check on the basic stiffness matrix of the structure. Any warning or error messages in the LUSAS output file should be investigated. See the additional section on LUSAS Output Errors in this User area for more information
- Check the output file for any warnings or errors from the eigen-buckling analysis
- If using the
- Subspace eigensolver
- , increase the number of starting iteration vectors. If any of the modes are close together the default magnitude for this parameter may not be sufficient to allow accurate resolution in their extraction. Increasing this parameter is also essential if only requesting a small number of eigenvalues (1-10)
- Ensure that the solution converged correctly. If not, and the solution history was converging, then increase the number of iterations permitted
- Tighten the convergence tolerance, since some modes may be close together and require greater numerical resolution. This may also require an increase in the number of iterations permitted
- Negative eigenvalues computed during an eigenvalue buckling analyses can imply genuine numerical difficulties in the solution procedure which can be rectified by using the
- alternative
- eigenvalue
- buckling
- solution in which the original buckling problem is recast to an alternative form in which, if certain rules are adhered to, all the computed eigenvalues will be positive (see the theory manual for more information). The alternative buckling procedure is available for the
- subspace
- iteration
- eigensolver
- and can be invoked from the advanced button on the eigenvalue control form as
- solve for 1/(1-buckling load).
- Because the load factor for alternative buckling is calculated from 1/(1-eigenvalue), negative eigenvalues can still be computed. From the equation, this can be seen to indicate that the applied load is higher than the buckling load, hence producing buckling load factors of less than unity - typically in the range 0.95-0.99. As a result, the load level must be specified to ensure that the load factors calculated are
- close
- to, but
- greater
- than unity (i.e. the load applied should be slightly less than the buckling load). The reason for the recommendation of ensuring that the load factors are
- close
- to unity is that if the load factor is too large, the eigenvalue will be approaching unity which implies that a small error in the eigenvalue may produce a large error in the computed load factor.
- Reduce the load applied to ensure that it is below the lowest expected buckling mode of the structure
- Negative eigenvalues can indicate bifurcation in tension or bifurcation that would occur if the loading is reversed in sign, i.e. the applied loading is in the opposite direction to that which would cause buckling of the structure
- Mechanisms can be excited in Semiloof shell (QSL8, TSL6) elements than give rise to negative eigenvalues – particularly for thin, cylindrical structures. The use of the fine integration rule for these elements will overcome such element mechanisms
- In certain situations the eigenvalues of the recast solution may be very closely spaced and cause convergence problems in the iterative solution. The number of permitted iterations should be increased from the default in this situation.
- Some shear dominated buckling analyses cannot be solved using the subspace method (even with alternative buckling) and the recommendation is then to use either the fast block Lanczos solver (if your licence allows) or the
- inverse
- iteration
- range
- method in which a range of eigenvalues or frequencies is specified in which the eigensolution will be computed.
- Note
- that the number of eigenvalues field should be specified as zero to ensure that all eigenvalues within the specified range are computed. Specifying a non-zero value that is less than the actual number in the range will not necessarily provide the lowest eigenvalues, i.e. there may be gaps
- From < https://www.lusas.com/protected/instruct/negative_eigenvalues.html >

- A BIM Execution Plan (BEP) provides a foundation to ensure deployment and delivery of a project is successful. It outlines the project specific approach to digital implementation including:
- Digital authoring tools of choice
- Project goals and objectives
- Project specific standards and guidelines
- Project coordinate system and base points
- Level of Development
- Applicable BIM Uses
- Project team roles and responsibilities
- Exchange Information Requirements
- A comprehensive version of the WSP BIM Execution Plan template is available and ready for use.

- This is a general rule of thumb breakdown and a blend of roles may be most suited to a WSP project.
- Drawing Mark-up Protocol
- WSP NZ - Project - Drawing Markup Cheat Sheet
- The draughtsperson is to carry out a self-check to ensure that what has been drawn/detailed & compared against current drawings does not include any major or minor issues i.e. duplicated section/elevation marks, clarity of drawings, consistency etc. Once complete, send a dated copy to the engineer and a drawing verifier for checking. For ease of communicating the markups, use the provided document as a guide.
- Level of Development (LOD)
- BIM+Handbook_(APPENDIX_C).v3
- This is a term used within the industry to define the extent and accuracy to which a building information model has been populated and the subsequent extent to which project team members may rely on it.
- The LOD requirement of a project is captured within the project specific BIM Execution Plan and LOD matrix and is dependent on the BIM uses associated with the model. All project members should be acutely familiar with the requirements specific to their project and as outlined within the abovementioned project specific documents.

- In

- .

- fields are the X and Y coordinates
- .
- Set CRS: in search type 2193>
- In processing toolbox search for Concave Hull
- Uncheck the allow holes
- Set the threshold to 0.03
- Output

- Format > autodesk dxf
- Geometry type> Polygon
- Import into CAD
- To get poly line
- HATCHGENERATEBOUNDARY

**To have points in actual colour**
- 2. Save It as a Real Vector Layer
- Right-click
- the new layer →
- Export > Save Features As

**In the export dialog**
- Format
- : Choose GeoPackage (recommended) or ESRI Shapefile
- File name
- : e.g., colored_points.gpkg
- ✅
- CRS
- : Set to EPSG:2193 (NZTM2000)
- Leave others default
- Click
- OK
- 3. Add the Saved Layer
- QGIS may automatically load it after export.
- If not, use
- Layer > Add Layer > Add Vector Layer
- and browse to your saved .gpkg or .shp.
- : Apply RGB Styling to Points
- Once it’s added to the map (you’ll see groundpoints),
- right-click it
- →
- Properties
- Go to
- Symbology
- In the
- Symbol panel

****
- Click the
- color swatch
- Click the
- Expression (ε)
- next to Fill Color
- Paste this expression: qgis CopyEdit color_rgb("field_4", "field_5", "field_6") (or rename fields if you've assigned names like R, G, B)
- Click OK → OK again
- To select the footpath points: Select by expression
- abs("field_5" - "field_6") < 30 AND
- abs("field_6" - "field_7") < 30 AND
- abs("field_5" - "field_7") < 30 AND
- "field_5" BETWEEN 50 AND 230
- Save the selected
- Right click on the point layer> Export> Save selected features As
